bucketBeginnerGuide.title: Build-A-Bucket Beginner Guide
bucketBeginnerGuide.intro: Build-A-Bucket turns NBA players into parts of one custom basketball star. Each wheel result gives you a new decision: use that player for the category they dominate, save a flexible category for later, and avoid finishing with a major weakness that lowers the completed build.

steps.title: Choose a Game Mode
steps.description: Start with the main wheel run for the complete randomized challenge, or use Sandbox Mode to test combinations more freely. A normal run makes you react to NBA players produced by the wheel and ends with a season simulation.
steps.tip: Use the normal wheel run first so you understand why saving elite specialists matters.

steps.title: Learn the Nine Skill Categories
steps.description: Your completed player is built from Finishing, Mid-range, Threes, Passing, Ball Handling, Rebounding, Post D, Perimeter D, and Athleticism. Every category needs one NBA player selection.
steps.tip: Separate the categories into offense, creation, defense, and physical tools before spinning.

steps.title: Spin the NBA Player Wheel
steps.description: Spin to receive an NBA player, then review which unfilled category best matches that player's real strengths. The biggest name is not automatically the best choice.
steps.tip: Before selecting, check which categories are still empty and which elite roles are hardest to replace.

steps.title: Select One Skill From the Player
steps.description: Assign the generated player to one available category and keep every other category open for future spins. Use Stephen Curry for Threes, Nikola Jokic for Passing or Rebounding, Kyrie Irving for Ball Handling, and Victor Wembanyama for Post D.
steps.tip: Use specialists in their clearest category unless that category already has an elite selection.

steps.title: Build a Balanced Foundation
steps.description: During the first half of the run, secure at least one scoring category, one creation category, and one defensive category. A balanced opening gives you more recovery options when later spins produce weaker players.
steps.tip: Finishing or Threes can score, Passing or Ball Handling can create, and Perimeter D or Post D can defend.

steps.title: Save Versatile Stars
steps.description: Keep multi-category stars available for the slot that becomes hardest to fill. Nikola Jokic can solve Passing or Rebounding, Giannis Antetokounmpo can cover Finishing or Athleticism, and Victor Wembanyama can strengthen Post D, Rebounding, or Athleticism.
steps.tip: Do not spend a versatile star on a category that already has a strong specialist.

steps.title: Protect the Weakest Grade
steps.description: After every selection, review the completed slots and identify the category most likely to finish below the rest of the build. Raising the weakest slot is usually more useful than adding another elite grade to a strength.
steps.tip: When only three categories remain, prioritize avoiding a low grade over chasing another highlight attribute.

steps.title: Complete All Nine Categories
steps.description: Continue spinning and assigning skills until Finishing, Mid-range, Threes, Passing, Ball Handling, Rebounding, Post D, Perimeter D, and Athleticism are all filled. The final selections should complement earlier choices.
steps.tip: Use the final flexible player to repair the lowest-value remaining slot.

steps.title: Simulate the Season
steps.description: Submit the completed custom player and run the season simulation. Compare the final overall rating, category grades, individual production, team success, and season result.
steps.tip: Record the weakest category and change that decision during the next run.

steps.title: Avoid Common First-Run Mistakes
steps.description: Choose categories by player fit instead of reputation and keep the complete nine-slot build in mind. Common mistakes include using an elite shooter for Mid-range instead of Threes, spending a top passer too early, stacking scoring, ignoring defense, and leaving an important category for the final random spin.
steps.tip: Fill scarce specialties first and use versatile players as insurance.

quickTips: Judge every pick by player fit and remaining categories, not by reputation alone.
Reserve versatile superstars for the slot that becomes hardest to fill.
A balanced build survives weak wheel results better than a stacked one.
Protect your weakest grade before chasing another elite highlight.

bucketBestBuilds.title: Build-A-Bucket Best Builds
bucketBestBuilds.intro: No wheel run produces the same player pool, so the most reliable strategy is to follow a build structure rather than a fixed list of names. Each template identifies the categories that deserve elite selections, the categories that can accept average results, and the easiest way to recover from a weak spin.

builds.name: All-Around Build
builds.strategy: The build can score, create for teammates, defend multiple areas, and survive weak wheel results without depending on one category.
builds.recovery: Place an average player in Mid-range or the less important defensive category, then preserve elite options for Passing, Finishing, Threes, and Perimeter D.
builds.avoid: Do not chase top grades in several offensive categories while leaving defense, Passing, or Rebounding far below the rest.

builds.name: Scoring-First Build
builds.strategy: The player can create shots at every level and is less dependent on teammates during the season simulation.
builds.recovery: Use a weaker option for Rebounding or Post D, but keep Passing and Perimeter D high enough to prevent the player from becoming one-dimensional.
builds.avoid: Do not assign every elite offensive player to scoring categories if Passing, Ball Handling, and Perimeter D remain empty.

builds.name: Defensive Build
builds.strategy: Elite defense and rebounding reduce the pressure on every offensive category and give the simulated player several ways to affect a season.
builds.recovery: Keep the three defensive foundations intact and use the best remaining offensive player for either Finishing or Threes.
builds.avoid: Do not finish with elite defense but no dependable scoring category or Passing option.

builds.name: Playmaking Build
builds.strategy: Passing and Ball Handling connect the entire build, while Finishing and Threes force defenses to respect both drives and perimeter shots.
builds.recovery: Allow Rebounding or Post D to absorb the weakest player, then protect Passing, Ball Handling, and one elite scoring category.
builds.avoid: Do not use the best passer for Rebounding unless another elite Passing option is already secured.

builds.name: Interior Build
builds.role: Paint scorer, rebounder, and rim protector
builds.strategy: The build controls the paint on both ends, creates extra possessions, and can remain effective without elite Ball Handling or Threes.
builds.recovery: Use the weakest guard or wing in Mid-range or Ball Handling, but keep at least one respectable perimeter skill to avoid a completely paint-only profile.
builds.avoid: Do not use every elite big for Rebounding and Post D before securing a top Finishing selection.

bucketOvr99Guide.title: Build-A-Bucket 99 OVR Guide
bucketOvr99Guide.intro: A 99 OVR attempt is a category-management challenge. The goal is not simply to collect nine famous players; it is to extract the best possible Finishing, Mid-range, Threes, Passing, Ball Handling, Rebounding, Post D, Perimeter D, and Athleticism combination from the wheel sequence.

steps.title: Memorize All Nine Slots
steps.description: Plan for Finishing, Mid-range, Threes, Passing, Ball Handling, Rebounding, Post D, Perimeter D, and Athleticism before making the first selection.
steps.rule: Never judge a pick only by the current player. Judge it by the current player, the remaining categories, and the specialists that may still appear.
steps.risk: Forgetting a category can leave an elite build with one final low-grade slot.

steps.title: Create a Specialist Reservation Board
steps.description: Reserve recognizable specialists for the categories where they create the largest upgrade.
steps.rule: Stephen Curry belongs at the top of the Threes board, Kyrie Irving at the top of the Ball Handling board, and Victor Wembanyama near the top of the Post D board.
steps.risk: Using a rare specialist in an easily filled category removes one of the clearest paths to an elite grade.

steps.title: Protect Multi-Category Superstars
steps.description: Delay versatile-player decisions until the run reveals which category is becoming difficult to fill.
steps.rule: Nikola Jokic can repair Passing or Rebounding, Giannis Antetokounmpo can repair Finishing or Athleticism, and Kevin Durant can strengthen Mid-range, Threes, or Finishing.
steps.risk: Spending a flexible superstar too early can force an average player into a more important category later.

steps.title: Prioritize Scarce Elite Skills
steps.description: Take an elite result immediately when the player has an obvious category that few other wheel options can match.
steps.rule: Prioritize rare Threes, Passing, Ball Handling, Post D, and Perimeter D specialists before using similar players in broader categories.
steps.risk: Mid-range, Athleticism, and secondary scoring options are usually easier to repair with versatile players.

steps.title: Do Not Duplicate the Same Value
steps.description: Avoid placing multiple elite players into categories that produce the same offensive or defensive strength.
steps.rule: After securing elite Threes and Finishing, improve Passing, Ball Handling, or defense instead of adding another scorer with limited creation.
steps.risk: Duplicated strengths can produce impressive highlights while leaving the overall rating limited by a weak category.

steps.title: Raise the Lowest Grade First
steps.description: Once several categories are filled, compare the likely grades and direct the next flexible player toward the weakest slot.
steps.rule: Turning a lower category into an A-range category is normally more valuable than turning an existing A into another top grade.
steps.risk: One low grade can prevent a high overall even when several other categories are elite.

steps.title: Use a 99 OVR Priority Map
steps.description: Follow a category-to-player profile map instead of selecting by reputation.
steps.rule: Use an elite rim attacker for Finishing, a shot creator for Mid-range, a historic shooter for Threes, an elite creator for Passing, a specialist dribbler for Ball Handling, a dominant big for Rebounding, a rim protector for Post D, a stopper for Perimeter D, and an explosive two-way athlete for Athleticism.
steps.risk: A superstar used outside the skill that defines their game can return a lower grade than a less famous specialist.

steps.title: Handle the Final Three Slots Carefully
steps.description: When three categories remain, stop chasing perfect strengths and prevent any category from collapsing.
steps.rule: Use the most flexible remaining player in the hardest slot, the clearest specialist in the second slot, and leave the easiest category for the final spin.
steps.risk: Leaving Passing, Ball Handling, Post D, or Perimeter D until last can make the final result depend on an unsuitable random player.

steps.title: Review Before Simulating
steps.description: Check that every selected player matches the assigned skill and that the build contains scoring, creation, defense, rebounding, and athletic support.
steps.rule: A strong 99 OVR attempt should have no obvious throwaway category and no elite player assigned to a role another player could have filled just as well.
steps.risk: The season simulation cannot repair poor attribute allocation.

steps.title: Use Failed Attempts as a New Draft Board
steps.description: After the season simulation, identify the weakest grade and the selection that caused it.
steps.rule: On the next run, reserve a better player for that category and move the original selection to a role that matches their strongest NBA skill.
steps.risk: Repeating the same early assignment will repeatedly create the same late-run weakness.

steps.title: Avoid the Main 99 OVR Killers
steps.description: Eliminate preventable allocation mistakes throughout the run.
steps.rule: Do not choose by name alone, waste a specialist, consume every versatile star early, stack only offense, ignore Perimeter D, leave Passing until last, or accept a severe weak grade without trying to repair it.
steps.risk: These mistakes lower the ceiling of the completed player before the final spin occurs.

bucketAttributeTierList.title: Build-A-Bucket Attribute Tier List
bucketAttributeTierList.intro: The strongest attributes are the ones that create value across many season outcomes or are difficult to repair late in a wheel run. Lower-tier placement does not mean an attribute is useless; it means that category can more safely accept an average player when a more important slot still needs an elite specialist.

tiers.label: Core Build Drivers
tiers.description: These categories should receive elite or near-elite players in most build styles because they create offense, prevent major defensive weaknesses, or influence many possessions.

attributes.name: Passing
attributes.why: Passing turns individual scoring skills into a complete offensive role and gives the season simulator another way to reward the player beyond points.
profiles: Elite lead guards and point forwards

attributes.name: Finishing
attributes.why: Finishing supplies efficient scoring around the basket and supports scoring-first, all-around, playmaking, and interior builds.
profiles: Powerful rim-attacking guards, wings, and bigs

attributes.name: Perimeter D
attributes.why: Perimeter D prevents a complete build from becoming an offensive specialist with no answer for opposing guards and wings.
profiles: Two-way wings with strong on-ball defense

tiers.label: High-Value Specialists
tiers.description: These attributes can define a build and deserve elite selections when they match the chosen play style.

attributes.name: Threes
attributes.why: Threes gives the player efficient perimeter scoring and complements Finishing, Passing, and Ball Handling.

attributes.name: Ball Handling
attributes.why: Ball Handling supports self-created scoring and combines especially well with Finishing, Mid-range, Threes, and Passing.
profiles: Primary ball handlers with low turnover risk

attributes.name: Rebounding
attributes.why: Rebounding adds possessions, supports defense, and creates reliable value even when the player is not scoring.

attributes.name: Post D
attributes.why: Post D gives the player interior resistance and pairs naturally with Rebounding and Athleticism.

attributes.name: Athleticism
attributes.why: Athleticism supports finishing, defensive coverage, transition play, and interior builds without replacing any specialized skill.

tiers.label: Flexible Allocation
tiers.description: These attributes remain useful but can more safely absorb an average wheel result when higher-priority categories still need specialists.

attributes.name: Mid-range
attributes.why: Mid-range adds another scoring level, but Finishing and Threes can already provide an effective inside-out scoring profile.

bucketBestPlayersWheelPicks.title: Build-A-Bucket Best Players and Wheel Picks
bucketBestPlayersWheelPicks.intro: A strong Build-A-Bucket run assigns specialists to the categories they dominate instead of choosing the biggest name every time. These recommendations combine the confirmed defensive categories shown in Sandbox Mode with the main offensive traits needed for a complete player, using official 2026 playoff statistics as comparison benchmarks.

picks.why: Brunson is a reliable choice when the build still needs a primary scorer who can create shots and carry a large offensive workload.
picks.skip: Use another Brunson trait when scoring is already covered and the build still lacks size, rebounding, defense, or passing.
picks.comparison: Choose Brunson for scoring specialization. Choose Gilgeous-Alexander when scoring and secondary playmaking are both valuable.

picks.why: Champagnie's postseason three-point volume makes him a useful specialist when the build needs dependable perimeter output.
picks.skip: Do not spend the pick here when another wheel option offers elite creation, defense, or rebounding and the build already has strong shooting.
picks.comparison: Both are volume-based shooting choices. Use the remaining build slots to add creation because made-three totals do not replace playmaking.

picks.stat: 86.3% shooting in the restricted area (2026 NBA Playoffs)
picks.why: Bridges provides an efficient finishing benchmark for builds that need better conversion around the rim.
picks.skip: Prefer a higher-volume interior scorer when the build needs frequent paint attempts rather than finishing efficiency alone.
picks.comparison: Choose Bridges for the highest displayed restricted-area efficiency. Choose Mobley when size and defensive value are also priorities.

picks.why: Jokic is the preferred playmaking pick because he creates offense at elite volume while also supporting scoring and rebounding combinations.
picks.skip: Save Jokic for rebounding when the wheel has already supplied an elite passer but no dependable rebounder.
picks.comparison: Choose Jokic for the highest passing benchmark. Choose Barnes when preserving Jokic for another premium category improves the complete build.

picks.why: Jokic is the strongest statistical rebounding option and is especially valuable when weak early wheel results have left the build undersized.
picks.skip: Use Jokic for playmaking instead when another elite rebounder is available and passing remains the larger weakness.
picks.comparison: Choose Jokic for maximum rebounding production. Choose Wembanyama when the build can combine rebounding with a later Post D or rim-protection decision.

picks.why: Wembanyama is the leading rim-protection benchmark and should be prioritized when the build lacks interior size or defensive resistance.
picks.skip: Use Wembanyama for another size-based category when Post D is already elite and a different wheel option can protect the rim.
picks.comparison: Wembanyama offers the higher block rate. Mobley is the fallback when Wembanyama's versatility is more valuable elsewhere.

picks.why: Eason's steal production makes him a strong disruption-based choice for a build that already has interior defense but lacks pressure on ball handlers.
picks.skip: Choose a more complete offensive trait when perimeter defense is already covered by an earlier elite selection.
picks.comparison: Eason owns the higher postseason steal benchmark. Smart is a close alternative when Eason is unavailable.

picks.stat: 26.3 points and 4.3 fast-break points per game (2026 NBA Playoffs)
picks.why: Banchero combines transition pressure with high scoring production, making him valuable when the build needs strength, downhill creation, and open-court offense.
picks.skip: Prioritize defense or playmaking when the build already has multiple explosive scorers but remains incomplete in other areas.
picks.comparison: Choose Banchero for a broader scoring profile. Choose Durant when the main goal is maximizing the displayed fast-break scoring benchmark.

bucketSandboxModeGuide.title: Build-A-Bucket Sandbox Mode Guide
bucketSandboxModeGuide.intro: Sandbox Mode works best as a testing laboratory rather than a normal wheel run. Start with one repeatable build, change only one category at a time, and compare the displayed grades, overall rating, and season performance before making another change.

steps.title: Choose One Build Goal
steps.description: Decide whether the test is focused on scoring, defense, playmaking, interior dominance, or overall balance.
steps.experiment: Begin with a clear target such as an elite defensive player instead of selecting unrelated star traits.
steps.readout: A focused target makes it easier to identify which selection improved or weakened the final build.

steps.title: Create a Baseline Build
steps.description: Assemble one balanced combination and treat it as the control build for every later comparison.
steps.experiment: Use a scorer, a playmaker, a rebounder, an interior defender, and a perimeter defender rather than stacking every slot into one strength.
steps.readout: Record the category grades and overall rating before replacing any player trait.

steps.title: Change One Selection at a Time
steps.description: Replace only one category while keeping the rest of the build unchanged.
steps.experiment: Compare Nikola Jokic and Scottie Barnes for playmaking while preserving identical selections in every other category.
steps.readout: Any rating or simulation change can then be connected directly to the replaced pick.

steps.title: Compare Specialists and All-Around Stars
steps.description: Test a category specialist against a player who contributes across several statistical areas.
steps.experiment: Compare Jalen Brunson's scoring with Shai Gilgeous-Alexander's combination of 27.6 points and 7.9 assists per game.
steps.readout: The specialist should win a narrow category test, while the versatile player may produce the stronger complete build.

steps.title: Run a Defensive Stack Test
steps.description: Combine elite rebounding, Post D, and Perimeter D selections, then identify the weakest remaining grade.
steps.experiment: A shared Sandbox result displayed Rebounding A+, Post D A+, Perimeter D A+, and Athleticism B+.
steps.readout: Use the lowest visible grade as the next category to improve instead of replacing an existing top trait.

steps.title: Test Category Synergies
steps.description: Pair traits that reinforce each other instead of evaluating every category in isolation.
steps.experiment: Test Jokic playmaking with Brunson scoring, Jokic rebounding with Wembanyama Post D, and Eason Perimeter D with a strong transition scorer.
steps.readout: Look for combinations that improve both the overall rating and the simulated season profile.

steps.title: Stress-Test Extreme Builds
steps.description: Create one offense-heavy build and one defense-heavy build using the strongest available specialists.
steps.experiment: Compare a Brunson-led scoring build against a Wembanyama, Jokic, and Eason defensive foundation.
steps.readout: The contrast reveals how strongly the simulator rewards balance compared with one-category dominance.

steps.title: Repeat the Season Simulation
steps.description: Simulate each controlled version and compare the final overall rating with its season outcome.
steps.experiment: Keep a simple log containing the changed category, selected player, final grade, overall rating, and season result.
steps.readout: Repeated tests turn Sandbox Mode into a practical decision guide for future wheel runs.

bucketSeasonSimulatorGuide.title: Build-A-Bucket Season Simulator Guide
bucketSeasonSimulatorGuide.intro: After the custom player is complete, Build-A-Bucket simulates a season to show how the combined traits perform. Read the overall rating together with the resulting player profile: a strong scorer may still need passing or defense, while a lower-scoring build can create value through rebounding, rim protection, and perimeter disruption.

profiles.inputs: Elite scoring and shooting selections led by players such as Jalen Brunson and Julian Champagnie.
profiles.benchmark: Brunson averaged 28.4 points per game, while Champagnie made 61 three-pointers in the 2026 playoffs.
profiles.read: A strong outcome should show that the player can carry a large offensive role without the complete build collapsing in non-scoring categories.
profiles.strong: High overall rating combined with a productive season and no severe defensive or playmaking weakness.
profiles.weak: Strong individual scoring but a weaker complete-season outcome.
profiles.adjustment: Replace the least valuable extra scoring trait with playmaking, Post D, or Perimeter D.

profiles.inputs: Nikola Jokic playmaking combined with reliable scoring and shooting.
profiles.benchmark: Jokic averaged 9.5 assists and 13.2 rebounds per game in the 2026 playoffs.
profiles.read: This profile should create broad value rather than relying only on points.
profiles.strong: The simulated player performs well across several areas and supports a strong overall season.
profiles.weak: Passing is excellent, but the build lacks enough scoring pressure or defense to convert creation into results.
profiles.adjustment: Add a primary scorer or improve the lowest defensive grade.

profiles.inputs: Victor Wembanyama Post D with an elite rebounder.
profiles.benchmark: Wembanyama averaged 3.5 blocks and 10.9 rebounds per game in the 2026 playoffs.
profiles.read: A successful result should reflect strong paint protection and possession control even when scoring is not the highest category.
profiles.strong: Elite defensive grades are supported by enough offense to produce a competitive season.
profiles.weak: The player protects the rim but cannot create or finish enough offense.
profiles.adjustment: Preserve Post D and replace a secondary defensive trait with scoring or playmaking.

profiles.inputs: Tari Eason or Marcus Smart Perimeter D with interior support behind them.
profiles.benchmark: Eason averaged 2.5 steals per game and Smart averaged 2.4 in the 2026 playoffs.
profiles.read: This profile should generate defensive value away from the rim and complement a separate Post D selection.
profiles.strong: Perimeter defense improves the complete result without creating a major offensive gap.
profiles.weak: The build disrupts opposing guards but remains too limited as a scorer or creator.
profiles.adjustment: Add an efficient offensive category while retaining the strongest perimeter defender.

profiles.inputs: Top Rebounding, Post D, Perimeter D, and a lower Athleticism grade.
profiles.benchmark: Three top defensive grades plus a B+ Athleticism were displayed in a shared Build-A-Bucket Sandbox result.
profiles.read: The three elite defensive categories form a strong foundation, while Athleticism is the clear improvement target.
profiles.strong: The season simulation rewards the defensive stack and the remaining offensive selections provide enough balance.
profiles.weak: The player earns excellent defensive grades but produces a limited overall or season result.
profiles.adjustment: Keep the three elite defensive categories and upgrade Athleticism or offensive creation.

profiles.inputs: One specialist each for scoring, playmaking, rebounding, Post D, Perimeter D, and Athleticism.
profiles.benchmark: Jokic led the displayed playoff benchmarks in assists and rebounds, while Wembanyama led in blocks.
profiles.read: Balanced builds should be judged by the absence of a damaging weakness rather than by one extreme category grade.
profiles.strong: Consistent grades, a high overall rating, and a strong simulated season.
profiles.weak: Every category is acceptable, but none is strong enough to drive elite results.
profiles.adjustment: Upgrade the most influential mid-tier category to a specialist without creating a new weakness.

profiles.inputs: Premium specialists assigned to their strongest categories with minimal duplicated value.
profiles.benchmark: The gameplay challenge Can I Create a 99 OVR on Build a Bucket? confirms 99 OVR as a featured build target.
profiles.read: A 99 OVR attempt should maximize category fit rather than simply collecting famous players.
profiles.strong: Elite grades remain balanced enough to reach the top overall-rating range and produce a dominant simulation.
profiles.weak: Several star selections overlap in the same strengths and leave one category far behind.
profiles.adjustment: Replace the most redundant star trait with a specialist for the lowest grade.

bucketUpdatesNewFeatures.title: Build-A-Bucket Updates and New Features
bucketUpdatesNewFeatures.intro: Build-A-Bucket launched as the basketball follow-up to Build-A-Player, using NBA player wheels, individual skill selections, custom builds, and season simulation. This timeline records each documented release and feature showcase from the original creator accounts.

entries.title: Build-A-Bucket Official Launch
entries.summary: Build-A-Bucket went live as a basketball version of the Build-A-Player format.
changes: Added a wheel containing NBA players.
Players select one aspect of each generated NBA player's game.
Selections continue until a complete custom basketball player is created.
The completed build can be tested through a season simulation.
The game launched through the existing Build-A-Player website.

entries.title: Sandbox Mode Feature Showcase
entries.summary: ZeroChillSports highlighted Sandbox Mode one day after the official launch.
changes: Confirmed Sandbox Mode as an available Build-A-Bucket experience.
Showed individual letter grades for build categories.
The shared result displayed top grades for Rebounding, Post D, and Perimeter D.
Athleticism appeared as the clearest remaining improvement target.

faq.title: Frequently Asked
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about Build-A-Bucket

questions.question: What is Build-A-Bucket?
questions.answer: Build-A-Bucket is a free browser basketball game. You spin a wheel of NBA players, choose one skill from each result, assemble a custom star across nine attribute categories, and simulate a full season.

questions.question: How do you play Build-A-Bucket?
questions.answer: Spin the NBA player wheel, assign each generated player to the category they dominate, fill all nine skill slots, and run the season simulation to see how your custom Build-A-Bucket player performs.

questions.question: What is Sandbox Mode in Build-A-Bucket?
questions.answer: Sandbox Mode is a Build-A-Bucket testing mode that lets you freely change category grades and player selections so you can compare combinations before committing to a normal wheel run.

questions.question: How do you reach a 99 OVR in Build-A-Bucket?
questions.answer: A 99 OVR in Build-A-Bucket comes from matching elite NBA players to their strongest categories, protecting scarce skills, avoiding duplicated strengths, and raising your lowest grade before the final spin.

questions.question: Where can I play Build-A-Bucket?
questions.answer: Build-A-Bucket runs in any modern browser through the official Build-A-Player website. No download, payment, or Roblox client is required.

questions.question: Is Build-A-Bucket getting updates?
questions.answer: Yes. Build-A-Bucket launched through the Build-A-Player format and Sandbox Mode was showcased the following day. New players, modes, and feature additions appear on the official Build-A-Player X account.

cta.title: Ready to Build Your NBA Star?
cta.description: Spin the player wheel, pick the best attributes, and chase a 99 OVR in Build-A-Bucket. Follow official updates on X and jump into the game in your browser.

footer.title: Build-A-Bucket Wiki

guide.title: Build-A-Bucket Guide

guide.metaTitle: Build-A-Bucket Guide - How to Play, Build Order & Tips
guide.metaDescription: Step-by-step Build-A-Bucket guide covering how to spin the wheel, choose skills, complete all nine categories, and run your first season simulation.
guide.whatIsDescription: Guides explain the core Build-A-Bucket loop: spin the NBA player wheel, assign each player to one of nine skill categories, complete a custom star, and simulate a full season. They cover first-run rules, mode selection, and the mistakes that ruin a build.

whySteps.step1Description: Understand the nine skill categories and how each wheel pick fills one slot on your custom player
whySteps.step2Description: Stop wasting elite specialists and leaving key categories to the final random spin
whySteps.step3Description: Follow a clear path from the first spin to a completed player and a full season simulation

builds.title: Build-A-Bucket Builds
builds.description: Complete player build templates for Build-A-Bucket: all-around, scoring-first, defensive, playmaking, interior, and weak-wheel strategies that survive bad spins.
builds.metaTitle: Build-A-Bucket Builds - Best Player Templates & Strategies
builds.metaDescription: Browse Build-A-Bucket builds including all-around, scoring, defensive, playmaking, and interior templates plus weak-wheel recovery and 99 OVR strategies.
builds.whatIsDescription: Builds are complete player templates that name the categories worth elite selections, the slots that can absorb average results, and the recovery plan when a wheel spin produces a weak option.

whySteps.step1Description: Each build shows exactly which categories to prioritize instead of guessing on every spin
whySteps.step2Description: Templates reveal where to place a weak player so the full build keeps its strength
whySteps.step3Description: Use category-allocation builds to chase the strongest possible overall rating

skills.title: Build-A-Bucket Skills
skills.description: Every skill category in Build-A-Bucket explained: Finishing, Mid-range, Threes, Passing, Ball Handling, Rebounding, Post D, Perimeter D, and Athleticism.
skills.metaTitle: Build-A-Bucket Skills - Nine Attribute Categories Explained
skills.metaDescription: Learn each Build-A-Bucket skill category, what it does, which NBA players fit it best, and how to prioritize the nine attribute slots.
skills.whatIsDescription: Skills are the nine attribute categories that make up every Build-A-Bucket player: Finishing, Mid-range, Threes, Passing, Ball Handling, Rebounding, Post D, Perimeter D, and Athleticism. Each category receives one NBA player selection from the wheel.

whySteps.step1Description: Know which skills are hard to repair late and deserve elite picks early
whySteps.step2Description: Assign each NBA player to the slot where they create the largest upgrade
whySteps.step3Description: Combine offense, creation, defense, and physical tools without a weak slot

wheel.title: Build-A-Bucket Wheel
wheel.description: Build-A-Bucket NBA player wheel strategy: spin mechanics, best picks per category, random player challenge tactics, and how to read each wheel result.
wheel.metaTitle: Build-A-Bucket Wheel - NBA Player Picks & Spin Strategy
wheel.metaDescription: Master the Build-A-Bucket wheel with spin strategy, the best NBA player pick for each skill category, and random player challenge tips.
wheel.whatIsDescription: The wheel is the core Build-A-Bucket mechanic. Each spin produces an NBA player, and you assign that player to one unfilled skill category based on their real strengths until all nine slots are complete.

whySteps.step1Description: Pick the category a player dominates, not just the biggest name on the spin
whySteps.step2Description: Hold multi-category players for the slot that becomes hardest to fill
whySteps.step3Description: Use wheel strategy to thrive even when every spin is unpredictable

players.title: Build-A-Bucket Players
players.description: Every NBA player in the Build-A-Bucket pool: tier rankings, strengths per category, legend players, and the best pick for each skill slot.
players.metaTitle: Build-A-Bucket Players - Tier List & Best NBA Picks
players.metaDescription: Explore the Build-A-Bucket NBA player pool with tier lists, the best players per skill category, and legend picks for every build.
players.whatIsDescription: Players are the NBA stars in the Build-A-Bucket wheel pool. Each player has category strengths that make them a better fit for some skill slots than others, and tier lists rank them by how much value they add to a complete build.

whySteps.step1Description: Find the strongest NBA player for each of the nine skill slots
whySteps.step2Description: Rank the pool by how much value each player adds to a complete build
whySteps.step3Description: Spot the versatile stars who can rescue multiple categories when spins go wrong

ratings.title: Build-A-Bucket Ratings
ratings.description: Build-A-Bucket overall rating explained: the OVR formula, how category grades combine, the 99 OVR target, and a rating breakdown for every build.
ratings.metaTitle: Build-A-Bucket Ratings - OVR Formula & Rating Breakdown
ratings.metaDescription: Understand Build-A-Bucket ratings: how the overall rating is calculated, how each category grade contributes, and how to push toward the maximum OVR.
ratings.whatIsDescription: Ratings measure a completed Build-A-Bucket player. The overall rating (OVR) combines the nine category grades, and the highest target is 99 OVR. Understanding the formula shows which categories move the rating the most.

whySteps.step1Description: See how each category grade feeds the final overall rating
whySteps.step2Description: Raise your lowest category because one low grade caps the entire rating
whySteps.step3Description: Apply the allocation strategy that reaches the top overall rating

simulation.title: Build-A-Bucket Simulation
simulation.description: Build-A-Bucket season simulation explained: regular season results, playoff performance, championship odds, season stats, and awards.
simulation.metaDescription: Read Build-A-Bucket season simulations: regular season records, playoff runs, championship odds, player stats, and end-of-season awards.
simulation.whatIsDescription: Simulation runs a full season for your completed Build-A-Bucket player. It produces regular season results, a playoff bracket, championship odds, and season stats so you can see how the build performs beyond the overall rating.

whySteps.step1Description: Interpret the regular season record and where the build succeeded or fell short
whySteps.step2Description: Follow the postseason path and championship odds for each build style
whySteps.step3Description: Link season output back to the skill categories that drove the result
